ontology.foodex2_description: Pastries and cakes — sweet filled pastry (baked)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for mooncake; A0CJW (sweet filled baked pastry) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Mooncake is a specific type of sweet filled baked pastry not individually enumerated in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-16.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for mooncake as a specific food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual baked mooncake (no yolk) weighs approximately 90 g, representing one standard serving as commonly sold in Singapore during the Mid-Autumn Festival.

nutrient_source.title: Mooncake, baked, lotus paste, no egg yolk — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F07030002 is sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database with confidence 0.95 and provenance marked canonical. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for mooncake (lotus paste, no yolk) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on sweet filled baked pastry with lotus paste and refined wheat flour
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for baked mooncake (no yolk) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is estimated by analogy with sweet baked pastries with refined wheat flour and high-sugar fillings (GI range 70–80 in University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (76 × 50.75 × 0.90) / 100 = 34.6 for a 90 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (35.4 g/100 g) with moderate sodium (117 mg/100 g); excess sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, independently elevating blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit baked mooncake to no more than 30 g per occasion and avoid pairing with other high-sugar or high-sodium foods, as the 35.4 g/100 g sugar load promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and contributes to blood pressure elevation per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 35.4 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate at 50.75 g/100 g with dietary fibre of only 2.66 g/100 g indicates a predominantly refined carbohydrate profile with high glycaemic index (~76), producing a significant post-meal glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid baked mooncake or restrict intake to a maximum of 30 g on special occasions with close blood glucose monitoring, as the combination of 35.4 g/100 g sugar, high GI (~76), and low fibre (2.66 g/100 g) will cause a rapid and substantial post-prandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100 g combined with high sugar (35.4 g/100 g) and total fat (17.32 g/100 g); high sugar intake is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit baked mooncake to occasional small portions (≤30 g) given the estimated saturated fat content of ~7.5 g/100 g and high sugar load of 35.4 g/100 g, both of which contribute to dyslipidaemia and elevated cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary recommendations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked mooncake (no yolk) is a traditional Chinese pastry consumed during the Mid-Autumn Festival, widely popular in Singapore and across Chinese communities in Southeast Asia. It consists of a thin, golden-brown baked wheat flour pastry shell encasing a dense, sweet lotus seed paste filling, with no salted egg yolk included. The pastry skin is made from wheat flour, golden syrup, alkaline water (lye water), and vegetable shortening or lard, giving it a characteristic soft, slightly chewy texture and a glossy, amber-coloured surface. The lotus seed paste filling is smooth, dense, and intensely sweet, contributing the majority of the food's sugar and caloric content. Per 100 g, baked mooncake (no yolk) provides 381 kcal, 5.56 g protein, 17.32 g fat, 50.75 g carbohydrate, 35.38 g sugar, 2.66 g dietary fibre, and 117 mg sodium. The food is energy-dense and high in refined sugar, making it clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 76 (high), producing a significant glycaemic load of approximately 34.6 per standard 90 g serving. Mooncakes are a seasonal festive food typically consumed in small portions during the Mid-Autumn Festival (农历八月十五); however, their high sugar and fat content warrants careful portion control. The food is vegetarian but not vegan (may contain lard) and contains gluten. It is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a baked mooncake without egg yolk?
What is the sugar content of baked mooncake?
Can I eat mooncake if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of mooncake?
Lotus paste mooncake calories per piece

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F07030002, provenance canonical, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry. GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database for sweet baked pastries with refined flour. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.5 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central data for analogous lotus paste mooncake (no yolk); HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This value should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific baked mooncake (no yolk) preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for baked mooncake (no yolk) is published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Glycaemic load per 90 g serving = (76 × 45.68) / 100 = 34.7, rounded to 34.6. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; traditional mooncake pastry may contain lard, which would render it non-halal. Consumers should verify with the manufacturer. 5. FoodEx2 code A0CJW is assigned as closest_parent for sweet filled baked pastry; no exact FoodEx2 code for mooncake exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2026-04-16. The foodex2_match_type has been set to 'closest_parent' to comply with the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with the validator-enforced allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. The previous value 'analogous_estimate' was not in the allowed set, causing g5_ontology_honesty to fail. Updated foodex2_gap_note to reflect the closest_parent designation. Updated notes field to document this correction.
